_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The EU Mobility Package I extension to light commercial vehicles places the workspace within the regulated operator tier of the EU road transport compliance chain as of 1 July 2026. The relevant regulatory taxonomy distinguishes three categories: (1) the transport operator holding a Community licence and bearing primary compliance responsibility for tachograph equipment, driving and rest time adherence, and posting declarations; (2) the driver carrying and operating tachograph cards and records; and (3) the consignor/freight forwarder whose instructions and load assignments may influence routing, scheduling, and posting triggers.

As a freight forwarder engaged primarily in air, road, and ocean movements across Americas/Europe/Asia trade lanes, the workspace typically acts as principal organiser of transport rather than registered road haulage operator. However, where the workspace sub-contracts European road legs to LCV operators — which is characteristic for last-mile, time-critical, and specialist deliveries across the live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, and high-value automotive verticals — it must verify that each sub-contracted carrier achieves full compliance from 1 July 2026. Failure by a sub-contracted LCV operator to carry a valid G2V2 tachograph, complete posting declarations, or respect driving-time limits can result in vehicle immobilisation at borders, directly disrupting time-sensitive consignments (event equipment, exhibition loans, film rushes, automotive reveals) where delay is commercially catastrophic.

For humanitarian cargo movements, where the workspace may organise rapid-response deliveries into or across EU Member States using chartered or sub-contracted vans, the same LCV rules apply unless the transport falls within a narrowly defined own-account exemption (transport not for hire or reward, carried out solely for the company's own operational needs per Regulation (EC) No. 561/2006, Article 3(ha)). Legal confirmation of own-account status for each humanitarian movement is required.

The workspace's role does not include direct obligations as tachograph installer or posting-declaration filer unless it operates its own registered LCV fleet. Where it acts purely as freight forwarder/agent, its obligations are contractual and due-diligence in nature: inserting compliance warranties into road haulage sub-contracts, auditing carrier readiness, and ensuring that routing instructions do not create schedules that would require drivers to violate rest-time rules. Role placement requiring legal confirmation: whether any workspace entity holds or requires a Community (international operator) licence for vehicles it may own or hire on a spot basis in the 2.5–3.5t band.

*Source: IRU, "EU cross-border transport rules extend to vans: what you need to know", IRU World Road Transport Organisation, May 2026. https://www.iru.org/news-resources/newsroom/eu-cross-border-transport-rules-extend-vans-what-you-need-know*
